questions.q: What is the difference between Monetary and Non-Monetary Items? Explain with examples. (a) Define Monetary and Non-Monetary Items, (b) Explain the accounting treatment of Monetary and Non-Monetary Items.
questions.answer: Monetary items are those that can be expressed in term of money and whose value can be measured in monetary terms. Examples of monetary items include cash, debtors, creditors, and bank overdraft. Non-monetary items, on the other hand, are those that cannot be expressed in terms of money or whose value cannot be measured in monetary terms. Examples of non-monetary items include goods, equipment, and property. Monetary items are accounted for at their historical cost, whereas non-monetary items are accounted for at their historical cost or their fair value, whichever is higher. For example, if a company purchases a piece of equipment for ₹10,000, it will be accounted for at its historical cost of ₹10,000. However, if the same equipment is sold for ₹15,000, it will be accounted for at its fair value of ₹15,000.
marking_scheme: Define Monetary and Non-Monetary Items (1 mark)
Explain the accounting treatment of Monetary and Non-Monetary Items (2 marks)
Provide examples of Monetary and Non-Monetary Items (2 marks)

questions.q: Distinguish between Material and Non-Material Subsidiary Items. Explain the accounting treatment of Material and Non-Material Subsidiary Items.
questions.answer: Material subsidiary items are those that are recorded in the books of account as they are significant in nature and have a material effect on the financial statements. Examples of material subsidiary items include wages, salaries, and rent. Non-material subsidiary items, on the other hand, are those that are not recorded in the books of account as they are not significant in nature and do not have a material effect on the financial statements. Examples of non-material subsidiary items include postage, stationery, and other small expenses. Material subsidiary items are accounted for by debiting the respective expense account and crediting the cash or bank account. Non-material subsidiary items are not recorded in the books of account as they are not material in nature.
marking_scheme: Define Material and Non-Material Subsidiary Items (1 mark)
Explain the accounting treatment of Material and Non-Material Subsidiary Items (2 marks)
Provide examples of Material and Non-Material Subsidiary Items (2 marks)

questions.q: Explain the concept of Capital Expenditure. Discuss the accounting treatment of Capital Expenditure.
questions.answer: Capital expenditure is the expenditure incurred by a business entity on long-term assets such as buildings, machinery, and equipment. It is also known as fixed asset expenditure. Capital expenditure is accounted for by debiting the respective asset account and crediting the capital account. The asset account represents the cost of the asset, while the capital account represents the increase in the company's capital. For example, if a company purchases a building for ₹10,000, it will be accounted for by debiting the building account and crediting the capital account.
marking_scheme: Explain the accounting treatment of Capital Expenditure (2 marks)
Provide an example of Capital Expenditure (2 marks)

questions.q: What is Accrued Income? Explain how it is accounted for in the books of account.
questions.answer: Accrued income is the income earned by a business entity but not yet received. It is also known as unearned income or deferred income. Accrued income includes rent, interest, and dividends. Accrued income is accounted for by debiting the respective income account and crediting the accounts payable or a suspense account. For example, if a company earns rent of ₹5,000 but has not yet received it, it will be accounted for by debiting the rent income account and crediting the suspense account.
marking_scheme: Explain how Accrued Income is accounted for (2 marks)
Provide an example of Accrued Income (2 marks)

questions.q: Explain the concept of Depreciation. Discuss the methods of calculating depreciation.
questions.answer: Depreciation is the decrease in the value of a tangible asset over its useful life. It is also known as the wear and tear of an asset. Depreciation is calculated using various methods such as Straight Line Method, Written Down Value Method, and Revaluation Method. The Straight Line Method assumes that the asset will lose its value evenly over its useful life. The Written Down Value Method assumes that the asset will lose its value at a faster rate in the early years. The Revaluation Method assumes that the asset will increase or decrease in value based on its future prospects. The depreciation expense is accounted for by debiting the depreciation account and crediting the asset account.
marking_scheme: Explain the methods of calculating depreciation (2 marks)
Discuss the accounting treatment of depreciation (2 marks)
